text: <par>Error in HPTB index: This is supposed to be a PKaren root (HPTB:260).</par>
<par>HPTB "Index of Proto-Forms" (p.599) has <latinform>*s-lay ⪤ *s-ləy</latinform>, which is an error (should be <latinform>*s-lay ⪤ *s-ley</latinform>).</par>

<par>Although STC sets up this root with <latinform>*-r-</latinform>, the evidence favors the reconstruction of <latinform>*-y-</latinform>. The only evidence for <latinform>*-r-</latinform> offered in STC is Bahing <latinform>moro</latinform> and the Intha dialect of Burmese (<latinform>mrok ~ mlok</latinform>) (see also Sunwar and Jirel <latinform>moro</latinform>). These Himalayish forms bear some resemblance to Nepali <latinform>mākul</latinform>, though they show no trace of a velar.</par>
<par>This allofam, poorly attested, was reconstructed in HPTB:39 on the basis of Bisu <latinform>mjò</latinform>, since plain nasals generally developed into Bisu stops.</par>

<par>Although Written Burmese does not differentiate between <latinform>nak</latinform> BLACK and DEEP, Lahu has a clear distinction: <latinform>nâʔ</latinform> 'black' < <latinform>*s-nakᴴ</latinform> vs. <latinform>ná</latinform> < <latinform>*ʔ-nakᴸ</latinform> 'deep'. This latter Lahu word has the high rising tone which developed by "glottal dissimilation" in syllables containing both a glottal onset and a stopped final (see Matisoff: 1970 passim and 1972, section 2.3).</par>
<par>The HPTB index erroneously classifies this form as PTB instead of *Burmish (see HPTB:26).</par>

<par>This is a root where some of the reflexes point to a unitary cluster of <latinform>*m + y</latinform>, while others have evidently treated the <latinform>*m-</latinform> as a prefix, yielding forms with initial <latinform>y-</latinform>.</par>
<par><plainlatinform>HPTB p. 31</plainlatinform> erroneously gives 1 as the PLB tone for this form.</par>
<par>Error in HPTB index: This is supposed to be a PKaren root (HPTB:305).</par>
<par>The <latinform>*d-</latinform> prefix was set up for PTB in HPTB on the basis of forms like Angami <latinform>te◦kwi</latinform>, but this is undoubtedly of more recent origin.</par>
<par>Recommend locating original source. <latinform>id</latinform> is suspicious, esp. given that gloss of previous form is 'id.'</par>
<par>AD #865 reconstructs MC <latinform>dźʼi̯a</latinform>. This OC reconstruction is a guess based upon the other characters in GSR #807.</par>
